insistence on the non-existence of the unconscious—on the brazenly spurious idea that mind extends only as far consciousness---is merely another tendentious rationalization, and a decidedly more sterile and superficial one than their false, but not entirely misconceived insistence on the primacy within people of rationality.
There is a second similarity between the obsessive-compulsive and the philosopher. They are both pathologically concerned with ethics—with questions of right and wrong and good vs. evil. In both cases, this is because they are crypto-criminals—people whose fundamental orientation with respect to others is anti-social—this being reflected in their solipsism and skepticism---but who also have strong, nay draconian, superegos and thus do everything in their power to suppress their criminal side. And to this end they enlist their pre-existing penchant for dwelling on technicalities of logic; which, when transferred from the sphere of knowledge-theory into the domain of ethics, results in their dwelling on technicalities of a legalistic nature. And this is why many an obsessive-compulsive, and also many a philosopher, goes into law.
Philosophers tend to be extremely sensitive to, and intelligent about, niceties of language.
This is obviously true of some obsessive-compulsives, but it is unknown whether it is categorically true of them. But supposing that it is, this, I think, is a derivative of their fear-based retreat from the external world, which is the realm of action, into the internal world, which is the realm of thought. After all, sensitivity to linguistic niceties being cut from much the same cloth as sensitivity to logical niceties, and the latter being, as we have already seen, a by-product of the philosopher’s and the obsessive-compulsives replacing of reality with cognitive proxies thereof.
One last point: I have noticed that a hugely disproportionate number of philosophers, including myself, are professional level musicians. Around 40%

A listener of mine, Emma, asked me: What are some early signs of OCD in children?
Here is my answer: First of all, all children are OCD. In fact, OCD is actually a prolongation into adulthood of the child's 'binary' emotional relation to the world. When you send a child to camp, he kicks and screams and cries. Then he has to come home, he cries again. Also, children, much like obsessive-compulsives, are soothed by repetition and like familiarity. They like to be told the same story at bedtime, night after night, and sleep with the same stuffed animal. That said, children who are actually OCD--i.e. who will be OCD as adults--do have some distinctinve characteristics. The main difference between them and non-OCD children is that they tend to have rituals and peculiarities that are specific to them. All children like to sleep with same stuffed animal night after and be told the same story night after night. But only OCD children have to sleep with a glass of water next to the bed or have to put their left shoe on before their first. Also, OCD children tend to be more cholicy and more emotionally intense and extreme than others children---more prone to bouts of depressive withdrawal. But I stress that OCD is never itself the issue, and OCD is not eliminated by being confronted head-on. OCD is about withdrawal from a frightening world into a safe space of thought and ritual, and the only way to conquer OCD is to conquer fear. Which one does by developing strengths of character and intellect. OCD is given rise to by emotional vulnerability, which in turn is given rise to by actual vulnerability--by defenselessness. And the way to defeat OCD is to give proper, non- neurotic defenses to the person in question.

the scope of my point about philosophy categorically being in the nature of an autistic withdrawal from reality.
But there is one last point to make, which does to some extent buttress my conception of philosophy as autistic retreat. Hume and Locke were arch-empiricists, meaning that, in their view, you don’t know it if you don’t see it (or otherwise have direct sensory evidence of it).
Now, empiricism collapses into the view that we can of nothing except our own conscious states; for the empiricist rejects all rules of inference would enable us to posit the existence of anything-
--the empiricist says that we can know it only if it is a datum of consciousness and, therefore, that if we have to posit it, meaning infer its existence, then it doesn’t exist.
So in their attempt to make philosophy engage the world, Locke and Hume empiricized philosophy. But in empiricizing philosophy, Locke and Hume made philosophy collapse into pure skepticism, pure skepticism being the most compete conceivable autistic withdrawal from reality into one’s own mind. And what this shows is that, even when philosophers try to make philosophy not be an autistic retreat, it ends up being precisely that. Which does rather confirm my conception of philosophy as autistic retreat.

Here is my answer:
There is only one way to 'get rid' of OCD, and that is to find a calling in life in which one's obsessive compulsive tendencies are an asset, as opposed to a liability. OCD is not so much an illness as it is a distinctive way of processing emotional information. In the wrong environment, that psychological architecture can hold one back; in right one, it can push one forward. Many writers, composers, mathematicians, lawyers, philosophers and creative and analytical people of many stripes (e.g. Beethoven, Einstein, Dr. Johnson) have OCD; such people have found callings in which their OCD works for them. So it's not about eliminating it; it's about putting it to one's advantage. I myself have done that in a very big way. I am being OCD right now, in writing such a thorough answer, but in being so thorough in this context, I am putting to my obsessive-compulsive tendencies to non-pathological use, instead of letting them express themselves as OCD. And that is point: one doesn't get rid of mental illnesses by getting rid of them; one chooses to work in areas in which those mental illnesses are functional and, therefore, not mental illnesses

What are Some Characteristics of OCD in Children?
A listener of mine, Emma, asked me: What are some early signs of OCD in children?
Here is my answer: First of all, all children are OCD. In fact, OCD is actually a prolongation into adulthood of the child's 'binary' emotional relation to the world. When you send a child to camp, he kicks and screams and cries. Then he has to come home, he cries again. Also, children, much like obsessive-compulsives, are soothed by repetition and like familiarity. They like to be told the same story at bedtime, night after night, and sleep with the same stuffed animal. That said, children who are actually OCD--i.e. who will be OCD as adults--do have some distinctinve characteristics. The main difference between them and non-OCD children is that they tend to have rituals and peculiarities that are specific to them. All children like to sleep with same stuffed animal night after and be told the same story night after night. But only OCD children have to sleep with a glass of water next to the bed or have to put their left shoe on before their first. Also, OCD children tend to be more cholicy and more emotionally intense and extreme than others children---more prone to bouts of depressive withdrawl. But I stress that OCD is never itself the issue, and OCD is not eliminated by being confronted head-on. OCD is about withdrawl from a frightening world into a safe space of thought and ritual, and the only way to conquer OCD is to conquer fear. Which one does by developing strengths of character and intellect. OCD is given rise to by emotional vulnerability, which in turn is given rise to by actual vulnerability--by defenselessness. And the way to defeat OCD is to give proper, non- neurotic defenses to the person in question.

needs an insane pretext to act sanely. The sane part of the obsessive-compulsive—the part of him that wants him to act---is like a parent who is trying to feed a toddler who refuses to eat, and the insane part of the obsessive-compulsive—the part of him that doesn’t want to act---is like the toddler. And the sane part of the obsessive-compulsive is, as it were, pretending that the spoon in his hand is an airplane and that the peas on the spoon are little passengers; and, in this way, by speaking the toddler’s language—his delusion-embodying language---the parent gets the toddler to do something good for himself.
So there it is. OCD is about refusal to act. It is about defensively holing oneself up inside consciousness. And getting the obsessive-compulsive to act---and thus to stop being obsessive-compulsive—is like a taking a cat out of a tree. It takes a lot of time and a lot of sensitivity. And the way to do is by exerting a firm and steady, but gentle pressure.
There is good news, thought. This cat in a tree that the obsessive-compulsive is---is sane. He knows that he is on a tree; he knows, deep down, that he has to get out of the tree. But he’s a bit gun-shy.
The obsessive-compulsive cat in a tree is not like the schizophrenic cat in a tree. The latter doesn’t know that he is in a tree or that he is a cat. And getting him to come down from three is dramatically more difficult, since, precisely because he doesn’t grasp reality as it is, there is no language in common between him and the person who is trying to talk him down.
But obsessive-compulsive sees things as they are. His problem is not deludedness; it is fearfulness. And since he does see reality as it is, one can use reason on him, as long as one does so gently, so as to not intensify his fears.

Institutional Psychopathy
A psychopath is someone who has no conscience. There is a phenomenon known as 'institutional psychopathization', this being the process whereby an entire institution becomes psychopathic, in the sense that the institution as a whole is without principle and also in the sense that the people composing it are without principle. Once an institution becomes just slightly psychopathized, it quickly succumbs to wholesale psychopathization. And a number of institutions and professions have become, or are in the process of becoming, psychopathic. This main reason for this is that, owing to technological advances, many institutions are not only obsolete and therefore serve no positive function, but actually thwart progress and prevent people doing what those same institutions are supposed to do. As a result, those institutions are fraudulent, and therefore psychopathic, and the same is therefore true of the people composing them, who have no choice but to being stone-psychopaths themselves if they want to be employed by those institutions. Many university-departments and academic disciplines are not completely and utterly psychopathic. This is glaringly where the humanities and social sciences are concerned. I believe that some branches of psychotherapy have become psychopathized, this being why psychiatrists are proverbially likely to make their patients’ problems even worse.
Because psychopathy is now being spread by psychopathic institutions and professions, it is dramatically more widespread than it used to be. Consequently, the old saw that only 1%-3% of the population consists of psychopaths is now pathetically outdated and also presupposes a narrow as well as antiquated conception of what a psychopath is. Most psychopaths in our time are not slick con-men but are rather bureaucrats, who discharge their psychopathy not by running Madoff-style swindles but by misdeploying educational and professional resources. I am hosting an ‘Ask me anything’

understood in terms of, that of a psychodynamic person . The bureaucrat’s architecture is to be understood in terms of that of the psychopath. The psychopath, like the bureaucrat, is incapable of internal conflict, since internal conflicts are mediated by conscience, and the psychopath has no conscience. The difference between the bureaucrat and the psychopath is that because the bureaucrat was once a person---because he was a person before his own decision-protocol was replaced by that of the corresponding institution---his psychological architecture, though non- psychodynamic and therefore effectively dead, has a formal similarity to that of the psychodynamic person: the bureaucrat’s psyche is the skeleton of a once existent but now dead and gone psychodynamic psyche. By contrast, the psychopath’s psyche does not even have a skeletal or formal similarity to that of a normal person.
There is no one way that bureaucrats look or sound. But they often have a distinctively nasal sound---a distinctly monotonic nasal sound. It sounds like this

The reason psychopaths can pass lie detector tests is that psychopaths don’t believe anything. So they don’t have to lie. In the psychopath, rationalizations have replaced beliefs. In other words, what it is convenient to believe has replaced what is actually believed. So nothing is believed. But certain propositions are accepted, as long as it is convenient to accept them.
The bureaucrat does not believe anything. But he accepts certain propositions because the bureaucracy that employs him requires him to accept them. In other words, he accepts those propositions because it is convenient to accept them.
Thus, in the bureaucrat, what it is convenient to believe has replaced what is actually believed. Rationalizations have replaced beliefs. Institutional pseudo-reality has replaced actual reality.
So what is the difference between the bureaucrat and the psychopath?
Ultimately, there is no difference. But so far as there is a difference, it is this: the bureaucrat is part of an organized collective of psychopaths, whereas the so-called psychopath is not a part of such a collective. So the psychopath is a rogue bureaucrat, and the bureaucrat is a non-rogue psychopath.
But if the bureaucrat were removed from his host-institution and were not quickly embedded in another bureaucracy, his behavior would immediately become that of the psychopath. The bureaucrat’s appearance of propriety and decency is just that—an appearance. There is nothing there: the bureaucrat is just as amorphous and insubstantial as the psychopath, but he has the external reinforcement of an institution. So it is this external reinforcement that gives the bureaucrat’s character any semblance of legitimacy or cohesiveness. And he knows this. He knows that, without this external reinforcement, he would turn into a protoplasmic blob of amorphous sub-humanity. So he knows that he has to march in lock-step with his host- institution, lest he turn into such a blob.
Thus, the bureaucrat depends not just for

And it thereby strips educational and psychiatric institutions of any reason to recognize it, and it gives them every possible reason to deny it when it happens and, therefore, to thwart the corresponding cases of intellectual growth.
Gardner’s theory is a pitiful and brazen rationalization of rigid and artificial purely institutional sub-divisions---of bureaucratic divisions of labor—that have only the most superficial correspondence to facts about the structure of intelligence. But educational and mental health-related institutions can certainly do quite enough to thwart intellectual growth in people as to make it seem as though Gardner’s pseudo-theory is correct. And I refer to it as a pseud-theory because that is what it is: it is a bureaucratic action that is dressed up as a psychological theory, with ruinous consequences.
And this is why OCD is on the rise. OCD has always has existed; that much is clear, given what a well-defined constellation of symptoms it involves. But OCD has definitely been on the rise in recent years.
Why is this?
OCD is what happens when one’s acceptance of legalistic, logical or pseudo-logical protocols is used to tamp down one’s emotions---one’s aggression, one’s sexuality, one’s vitality. And a spreading educational and legal bureaucracy—which is supported by fake experts, as Howard Gardner, with their fake and undermining theories, such as Gardner’s theory of intelligence---are making it increasingly difficult for people of great potential, and of correspondingly strong passions, to discharge those passions in a way that is constructive or, indeed, that is anything other than blind rage. And this rage is filtered through internal legalistic and pseudo-logical protocols that mirror the institutional protocols of the educational system in which the person in question is trapped. So, yes, there has been a rise in OCD. And that rise is in lock-step with the spread of cancerous educational and professional bureaucracies, and it is exacer

What is the Prognosis for People with OCD?
Somebody on Quora.com asked the following question:
What is the prognosis for patients with Obsessive–compulsive disorder (OCD)? Here is my answer:
People who have OCD have it because it provides them with relief from anxiety—from anxiety so intense as to be almost inconceivable to people who have not had OCD. But OCD is just a defense-mechanism. And as is the case with most defense-mechanisms, there comes a time in the subject’s life when it starts to do more harm than good. At that point, the obsessive- compulsive has to make a decision: What do I want? Do I want mere freedom from anxiety? Or do I want to live and grow? It is 100% up to him what he chooses. If he chooses to grow, he will, and his OCD, without exactly vanishing, will assume productive forms. If he chooses not to grow, he won’t.
OCD, though an affliction, is ultimately a voluntary one. If the obsessive-compulsive chooses to remain in his defensive bubble of isolation from the world, then his OCD will defeat him. If he chooses to re-engage the world, then his OCD will assume non-pathological and in fact productive forms, as it did with Beethoven and Einstein.
OCD, though an affliction, is an affliction of the will—and is thus unlike cancer or asthma, which have nothing to do with the integrity of one’s will. And OCD is eliminated, or at least made to assume adaptive forms by duly exercising one’s will in growth-oriented ways.
The symptoms of OCD are involuntary only in a relative sense. Relative to a determination to remain within his defensive bubble of non-engagement, there is nothing much that the obsessive-compulsive can do about those symptoms. He can engage in a bit of ‘cognitive-behavioral therapy’, but the effects of such therapy will be shallow and short-lived, absent a determination on the obsessive-compulsive’s part to grow and change himself in a way that makes his OCD irrelevant. On the other, if he does choose to leave his defensive bubble and

Charles Manson’s followers gave him everything: money, sex, labor, drugs, adoration. And what did give them? Nothing. Or almost nothing. He gave them one thing---and it was the most important thing of all: a purpose. He made them believe that they were part of an important cause. A person with a cause and no money has almost everything. A person with all the money in the world and no cause has almost nothing. And Manson gave his minions a cause, and they gave Manson everything else.
Jim Jones’ followers gave him everything. They provided him with slave labor. They barely slept. And at least until the last few months, they did all of this voluntarily. And what did Jim Jones give his followers? Nothing. Or just one thing—he gave them a sense of purpose. He gave them a cause that was their own.
These were profoundly evil men—consummate manipulators. But it was for that very reason that they knew exactly what people need. If they hadn’t known as much, they couldn’t have been such successful predators. And what people need, as these two knew, is a purpose.
People will put up with pain and strife if they have a purpose. And they won’t if they
don’t.
Getting rid of OCD is hard. If a given obsessive-compulsive has a purpose in life that his
OCD is preventing him from fulfilling, then he will get over his OCD. Otherwise he won’t. OCD is simply too painful to get rid of unless one really has to. And one really has to if, and for the most part only if, OCD is interfering with one’s purpose in life.
But there is deeper connection between having a purpose in life and getting rid of OCD. OCD is about having a bifurcated psychological structure. It is about being of two minds—not having a single hegemonic set of values to which all psychical activity is subordinate and, for this reason, not having any way of resolving the internal conflicts that are wrenching one’s psyche apart.
If one has a grand purpose in life, one has a way of resolving these conflicts; for if one has a master

People who have social anxiety don’t want to be around people. And they don’t want to be around people because they hate people, or at least are angry at them.
And notice that the effect of social anxiety is to keep the person who has it away from people. When he is around people, he is upset. When is alone again, he stops being upset. He tells himself that he fears others. And there is a certain truth in this. But really it is more that he fears that he might do them; he is afraid of the danger that his own anger towards others might put him in.
In this respect, social anxiety is similar to shyness. Shyness is ultimately about intense rage towards others. This becomes immediately obvious to anyone who has actually entered into serious communication with a shy person: what becomes clear---because they explicitly affirm as much—is how intense their hatred is of others.
In any case, people with social anxiety are not necessarily wrong to hate people. People with social anxiety are often highly intelligent and sensitive people who are misunderstood by others and have been wronged by them. So it is not necessarily incumbent on someone with social anxiety to change his views or feelings about others. But if he wants to function in society, it is incumbent on him to recognize why he has social anxiety. And once he acknowledges that he has it because of anger, his anger will become conscious. And once it becomes conscious, he can put it in its proper place. When anger is unconscious, it widens its target; it spreads indiscriminately, and people become the object of it who would otherwise be targeted by it. But when anger becomes conscious, it is confined to its rightful target--to the specific individuals who have wronged on---instead of being inappropriately applied to all people.
There are two ways for someone with social anxiety to get over it. The first is to acquire power. Hatred is about impotence, and it tends to vanish as one acquires power. The second is to accept

Structural Therapy
The preferred way of treating OCD these days is known as “cognitive behavioral therapy.” CBT be called “behavioral therapy” since there is nothing cognitive about it, as it consists entirely in changing the obsessive-compulsive’s behavior directly, and not way of changing the emotional conflicts responsible for his behavior.
Obviously, the obsessive-compulsive’s behavior does have to be changed; indeed, getting him to become at least minimally functional, and therefore getting him to not be completely crippled by his compulsions, is a prerequisite for him engaging in any sort of meaningful therapy or, indeed, in his having a meaningful life.
But taken by itself, behavior modification is not enough. Indeed, behavior modification, unless supplemented by treatment of underlying emotional disturbances, fails of its own purpose, as in, it fails even to modify behavior, since those emotional conflicts will always find new ways of pathologizing behavior.
The only real way to treat OCD is through structural therapy. Structural therapy is the process of changing the obsessive-compulsive’s mentality from that of someone who is reality- avoidant, and therefore fantasy-oriented, to being someone who is reality-oriented, and therefore fantasy-avoidant. The obsessive-compulsive’s obsessionality is about his preferring symbolic representations of reality to reality and, consequently, his preferring thought to action. And his compulsions are merely externalized obsessions and therefore represent overt expressions of his reality-avoidance.
In order to effect this change of orientation within the obsessive-compulsive, it is necessary to modify his behavior; and cognitive behavioral therapy is therefore a component of structural therapy. But it is a subordinate component, the main component being the just- described reversal in the obsessive-compulsive’s mentality.

I recently joined linked in, and I immediately connected with a number of OCD- therapists, all of whom claimed, in their about me sections, that they were looking for opportunities to broaden their therapeutic horizons and learn more about OCD. And I reached out to them, telling them to check out m website, OCD and philosophy and give me their feedback. And I invited a few to do podcasts with me, in which I would give them a chance to discuss their views. Not one responded meaningfully; they all responded by saying ‘thanks!’, and nothing else. In other words, they hit the auto-reply ‘thanks’ button at the bottom of the message.
And when I took a second look at them, I saw that they were timid looking, unadventurous, cautious and scared people. Not bad people, by any means. But not people who projected strength; not people whose presence suggested that they had ever overcome anything; not focused or centered people. But rather people who living life defensively, playing by a pre- existing rule-book, and just trying to keep their heads above water.
And that is understandable. But it means that as OCD-therapists, their batting rate is going to be zero. The essence of any therapeutic relationship is confidence on the patient’s part in the therapist; it is belief, in the competence and integrity, on the part of the analysand in the analyst. The analyst’s words affect the analysand by way of the rapport that exists between them, and that rapport is based on the analysand’s confidence in the analyst. And if that confidence isn’t there, the analyst’s words are not going to mobilize restorative energies within the patient. The analyst will retain the power to hurt the patient with his words, but he will not be able to do much in the way of helping the patient.
Psychoanalysis and psychotherapy are not exercises in computation. Obviously, intelligence and knowledge on the part of the therapist can help enormously; but they help only on the condition that there is an empathic r

How do I get over my OCD?
Somebody on Quora.com asked: How do I get over my OCD? Here is my response:
The right question is not ‘how do I get over OCD?’ so much as it is ‘in what sort of context is my OCD an asset, as opposed to a liability?’ People with OCD are capable of intense thought and prolonged concentration. They are able to be mindful of details without losing sight of the big picture. Their ‘cognitive wiring’ is capable of being an enormous asset. At the same time, in some contexts, that same cognitive wiring—that same intellectual acuity and emotional sensitivity—-may be liability. In contexts, in which the obsessive-compulsive is being subjected to extreme and untoward emotional pressures, his finely tuned psyche and hyper-responsiveness may result in his defensively withdrawing behind a veil of obsessions and compulsions, the ultimate purpose of which is to insulate him from a threatening and toxic external reality. There are thus two components to dealing with OCD: (i) Identifying contexts in which one’s OCD is a strength, as opposed to a weakness and (ii) taking advantage of such contexts to build up one’s self-confidence so that, in adverse contexts, one does not become fearful and withdraw into a protective (but dangerously self-limiting) cloud of obsessions and compulsions.

If you have a goal, it is easy not to be lazy. But if you don’t have goal. It is hard not to be lazy, since there is no point in not being lazy. Similarly, if you have a goal, it is easy not to succumb to neurotic compulsions. But if you don’t have a goal, it is hard not to do so since there is no point in tolerating the anxiety brought on by not doing so.
As for obsessions, these are always displacements: one obsesses about such and such as a way of not confronting thus and such. And when one has a goal, one’s obsessional energies are naturally channeled in the right direction, since, under that circumstance, one’s mind stands to gain more in the way of relief from anxiety by not obsessing than by obsessing. And when doesn’t have a goal, one’s mind stands to gain more in the way of anxiety-relief by obsessing than by not obsessing, and one is therefore inclined to keep on obsessing.
As for intrusive thoughts, these are one’s mind’s way of telling one—in a compressed, crude, and distorted way---what one has to do. Notice that one’s intrusive thoughts concern sex or violence or sexual violence. This is because the people who have such thoughts are in the process of castrating themselves—they are in the process of trying to become faceless bureaucrats---and their minds are recoiling at this violence that is being done to them, and they are shouting at one’s conscience mind, trying to tell it not to surrender. Intrusive thoughts are not the problem; the person who is making a eunuch of himself is the problem; the intrusive thoughts are the last gasp of the still robust part of one’s mind. In any case, the way to get over obtrusive thoughts is to have a goal in life. So long as one is drifting, one’s mind will be unsatisfied and will hiss and buck, sometimes by flooding one’s consciousness with intrusive thoughts or with compulsions, sometimes in other ways: but so long as one is drifting, it is just a matter of time before one’s psyche begins to disintegrate. And one c

All people are dominated by compulsions. In the case of the non-obsessive-compulsive, those compulsions are unconscious. In the case of the obsessive-compulsive, they are conscious. And that is what it is to have OCD: it is to consciously experience the compulsions that others only experience unconsciously. Thus, where the non-obsessive-compulsive has the illusion of freedom, the obsessive-compulsive has the illusion of its absence. In actuality, the obsessive- compulsive, by virtue of being aware of the compulsions to which he is subjected, has the ability to liberate himself from those compulsions, whereas the non-obsessive-compulsive, by virtue of not being thus aware, does not.

Physical vs. Mental Illness
Almost all physical illnesses are simply deficits—as in, they don’t do those who have them any good. And most mental illnesses are simply deficits. But OCD—though functionally a deficit—is an asset in two important ways. First, those who have it are highly intelligent, meaning that they are good at processing intellectual or cognitive information, and highly sensitive, meaning that they are good at processing affective or emotional information.
Philosophical aside: What we refer to as ‘sensitivity’ is a form of internal awareness—of inwardly directed intelligence: it is about different parts of the mind being in good communication with each other. And what we refer to as “intelligence” is outwardly directed intelligence; it is about being aware of dependence-relations holding among mind-external entities. In any case, the obsessive-compulsive has both kinds of intelligence; indeed, his condition is about those two forms of intelligence being hyper-operative---being over-deployed. So, yes, so long as they are over-deployed, he is seriously impaired; but his impairment has to do with the mutually cancelling effects of powerful cognitive forces, not, as is the case with most psychological impairments, with an absence of such forces.
And this relates to the second respect in which obsessive-compulsives are at an advantage.
Obsessive-compulsives are psycho-dynamic. They are internally conflicted. Which, though not an asset in and of itself, shows that they have ideals and, moreover, that they are taking themselves to task for falling short of those ideals. Where there is no conception of right and wrong—no conception of how, morally and otherwise, things should be—there is no internal conflict. And where there is a conception of right and wrong, there is ipso facto a willingness to try to make things right. And where such willingness exists, it can always be used by the person who has it to make himself better. In the case of the obsessive-com

One of my listeners, Earth Pet, asked the following question. I quote:
“How would you advise attenuating or controlling compulsions for those without the obsessive component?”
Here is my answer.
Obsessions are thwarted compulsions. In other words, when one has a compulsion that one is prevented from acting on, one is left with an obsession. Equivalently, compulsions are obsessions that are expressed in behavior.
So the relationship between the obsession and the compulsion parallels the relationship between intention and subsequent action.
And just as there is always an intention whenever there is an action, so there is always an obsession whenever there is a compulsions. Of course, the obsession does not become an object of consideration in its own right so long as it is discharged sufficiently quickly in action, just as an intention does not become an object of attention in its own right if sufficiently quickly discharged in action. But just as there is an intention whenever there is action, so there is an obsession whenever there is a compulsive action.
So the situation presupposed by the question does not in fact exist.
I will answer all questions in real time, and I will answer them in audio/video form, as I did with this question.
And please visit me at my website www.freudinstitute.com where you can pose questions and actually chat in real time about matters OCD-related matters with clinicians and scholars.

I was going for my nightly walk and, as is my wont, thinking about the various ventures that I am trying to get off the ground. And, as is also my want, I was plagued with doubts—with self-doubts. And it then occurred to me why psychopaths are so successful: they are not neurotic. Self-doubt is inherently neurotic. It is one thing to know one’s own limitations: that is not self- doubt; that is just plain self-knowledge. But self-doubt is not about self-knowledge or even
quasi-self-knowledge; it is moralistic self-undermining; it is neurotic self-punishment. And because psychopaths don’t have consciences, they don’t doubt themselves and, more generally, they are not neurotic. Neurosis is about a conflict between one’s primal desires and one’s conscience. So psychopaths, having as they do no conscience, are not neurotic; and they are therefore not held by neurosis or, consequently, by neurotic self-doubt or, consequently, by any self-doubt. Non-psychopaths, on the other hand, even exceptionally gifted ones—nay, especially exceptionally gifted ones—are riddled with neurosis and therefore with self-doubt and are therefore prone to self-sabotage. The ideal condition is to be a non-neurotic, non-psychopath; and that condition is achievable, and it is my objective as an analyst to get people attain that condition.

The purpose of psychoanalysis and indeed of all forms of psychotherapy is to get the patient to live in the real world. Freud himself knew this: he explicitly said that the sole functions of psychoanalysis were to get the patient to be able to work and to love.
At the same time, Freud said, or certainly seemed at certain junctures to say, that mental health is all about sex—that mental illness is all about not having a good sex life and that mental well-being is all about having a good one.
Which, if indeed he did say this, is seriously overstated, not positively erroneous.
But there is a way of interpreting this supposed conceit of Freud’s that gives it legitimacy.
Let’s start with the premise that sanity is about living in the real world and insanity is about living in one’s imagination.
To the extent that people are not actually having sex with other human beings, their sex lives are masturbatory and fantasy-based, and are therefore dissociated from reality and, quite literally, insane. And to the extent that people are having sex---provided that it is emotionally engaged sex, as opposed to just masturbation that happens to another person’s body---one’s sex life is reality-based, non- dissociated and, therefore, quite literally sane.
Is there more to having a non-dissociated and sane existence than having a non- dissociated sex life? Yes. Is there more to having a dissociated and insane existence than having a dissociated sex life? Yes. But one’s life is non-dissociated to the extent that one’s sex life is non-dissociated and dissociated others. Moreover, as Freud said, however overstatedly, one’s sex life is to some extent a prototype for the rest of one’s life, as in, there is at least some tendency for the non-sexual component of one’s existence to be non-dissociated if the sexual component of one’s existence is non-dissociated and dissociated otherwise.
To sum up: Sanity is about living in the real world. Masturbation is about not living in the real world. So

Autistic people seem to live in their own world, and the obvious thing to say about them, therefore, is that they are self-contained. But they are not self-contained. They are pseudo-self- contained. Autism is about needing people early in life and being given trauma instead. And the traumatized child reacts by withdrawing into himself. But his withdrawal is not about being emotionally self-sufficient. It is about quite the opposite. It is about being so dependent on others that unless one is given just the right cocktail of emotional support from others, one hysterically flees into psychotic seclusion. Consider the jilted bride who, instead of going back on the dating scene, continues to wear her wedding dress while pretending that the marriage actually happened. This person is psychotically secluded and therefore isolated from reality—but not
self-contained. Quite the opposite. And the same is true of the autistic.
Being genuinely self-contained is about balance. It is about being given—or having been given—the right level, and the right kind of emotional sustenance from others and, on that basis, being able, like a dolphin that has taken in enough air or a camel that has taken in enough water, to go out on one’s own. This is not what the autistic person is doing. Autism is a way of being damaged. True independence is about the opposite of being damaged. And a sign of true independence is flexibility, and rigidity is a sign of damage-based pseudo-independence.
One last point: Bureaucrats are people who are damaged and have no free will.
Bureaucrats need others to tell them what to do. And when they come across someone who is genuinely independently, they invidiously describe him as having ‘autism’ or ‘Asperger’s’, sometimes going so far as to get psychiatrists to formally diagnose them in this way. But such characterizations are, as we have just seen, the opposite of the truth---and, like many invidious characterizations, not just false but also projective.

There are two distinct ways in which reading increases intelligence. On the one hand, reading makes one aware of truths of which one was previously unaware, and each such truth is a new weapon in the arsenal of one’s intellect. On the other hand, reading stimulates thought, and its effect on one’s intellect is similar in this respect to the effect that physical exercise has on one’s body. When one exercises, a message is sent to one’s metabolism that it must build
muscle-tissue and make all of the other changes to itself that are required for an exercise- intensive existence. And when one reads or otherwise engages one’s intellect, a message is sent to one’s central nervous system that it must forge new neural pathways and make all of the other changes to itself that are require for a thought-intensive existence. So, reading is not simply about uploading new information. It is also about keeping one’s central nervous system constantly stimulated, so that it continues to keep itself combat-ready, so to speak, and able to take on new tasks and develop new capabilities. And just as one loses stamina, muscle-power and coordination if one does not engage in athletic training, so one loses neural plasticity and intellect if one does not engage in cognitive training. By reading and otherwise engaging one’s intellect, one keeps one’s mind nimble and elastic. Indeed, it is more by keeping one’s mind supple and active than it is by making one aware of new facts that reading promotes intelligence, and the same is true of other forms of intellectual activity. The primary intellectual benefit of vigorous intellectual activity is not that it stocks one’s mind with new ideas, so much as it is that it keeps one’s mind in a state of constant readiness and consequent responsiveness. And this heightened responsiveness, in its turn, makes one’s mind more able to acquire new information.

Somebody on Quora dot com asked the following question. I quote:
Do you believe mental illness is “an illness like any other” or is it a distinct and unique phenomenon? Why?
Here is my answer:
Mental illness is not an ‘illness like any other.’ Mental illnesses are contextual in nature.
In some contexts, being obsessive-compulsive is a liability and thus an illness, and in other contexts, being obsessive-compulsive is an asset and thus not a mental illness. And the same is true, to varying degrees, of all other mental illnesses. By contrast, physical illnesses are not contextual in nature: if somebody has asthma or lupus or cancer, that is not asset in some contexts and a liability in others: it is simply a liability. Also, mental illnesses serve a defensive function. Even though, in at least some contexts, OCD is a liability, it serves a protective function: it insulates the subject in a psychological ‘safe-space’: his obsessions and compulsions encircle that safe-space and, although dysfunctional in some respects, provide him some measure of safety from a toxic and threatening environment. And the same is true of psychosis: the psychotic did not become psychotic for no reason. He was in a situation in which he was being threatened and pumped full of negative validations and detriments to his sense of self-worth.
And he therefore decided to distance himself from that destructive negativity by withdrawing into a world of psychotic delusion. So even though psychosis is an illness and is some respects gravely dysfunctional, it does serve at least some protective function. Also, to develop a point hinted at earlier, some mental illnesses involve misdeployed strengths of intellect and character. OCD involves misdeployed intelligence and focus; schizophrenia involves misdeployed creativity and emotional sensitivity. So mental illnesses, though in many respects gravely debilitating, sometimes seem to involve heightened, albeit misdeployed, abilities.

Parents are getting psychiatrists to falsely diagnose their children as having autism, Asperger’s, ADHD, and the like. Such diagnoses—especially false diagnoses of Aspersers and Autism—are nothing more than cloaked child murder. The parent---usually the mother---wants her husband’s attention; can’t get it; and then, in an effort to get it—or to get some surrogate thereof—figuratively murders the child with a false psychiatric diagnosis, which dooms the child in question to the over-attentions of idiot educationalists, psychologists, psychiatrists, and bureaucrats---the effect of which is to damage the child in some way or other, sometimes going so far as damage him in a way that retroactively validates the initial diagnosis. Of course, with rare exceptions, psychiatrists and educational psychologists are none too bright and are all too willing to foist bunk diagnoses on hapless youths, both because they need money and also because they need to feel that their line of goods has some legitimate application, which it doesn’t. But rule of thumb: when a child is sent to a psychiatrist, the first place to look for psychological maladies is in the Munchausen-by-proxy mother’s head, not the child’s. The child’s mind is the place to look for fall out from the blast, not for the blast’s mom-centric epicenter.

Somebody on Quora.com asked the following question:
What is the prognosis for patients with Obsessive–compulsive disorder (OCD)? Here is my answer:
People who have OCD have it because it provides them with relief from anxiety—from anxiety so intense as to be almost inconceivable to people who have not had OCD. But OCD is just a defense-mechanism. And as is the case with most defense-mechanisms, there comes a time in the subject’s life when it starts to do more harm than good. At that point, the obsessive- compulsive has to make a decision: What do I want? Do I want mere freedom from anxiety? Or do I want to live and grow? It is 100% up to him what he chooses. If he chooses to grow, he will, and his OCD, without exactly vanishing, will assume productive forms. If he chooses not to grow, he won’t.
OCD, though an affliction, is ultimately a voluntary one. If the obsessive-compulsive chooses to remain in his defensive bubble of isolation from the world, then his OCD will defeat him. If he chooses to re-engage the world, then his OCD will assume non-pathological and in fact productive forms, as it did with Beethoven and Einstein.
OCD, though an affliction, is an affliction of the will—and is thus unlike cancer or asthma, which have nothing to do with the integrity of one’s will. And OCD is eliminated, or at least made to assume adaptive forms by duly exercising one’s will in growth-oriented ways.
The symptoms of OCD are involuntary only in a relative sense. Relative to a determination to remain within his defensive bubble of non-engagement, there is nothing much that the obsessive-compulsive can do about those symptoms. He can engage in a bit of ‘cognitive-behavioral therapy’, but the effects of such therapy will be shallow and short-lived, absent a determination on the obsessive-compulsive’s part to grow and change himself in a way that makes his OCD irrelevant. On the other, if he does choose to leave his defensive bubble and
re-engage the world, then, indeed, his OCD
